steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the main idea of the whole passage. The passage makes two moves: (1) Smith is famous for the invisible hand metaphor, but he used it only once and narrowly; (2) twentieth-century economists deliberately elevated it into a paradigm to back their own views. The main idea must capture both the fame and its true source.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — The reputation of Smith's metaphor of the invisible hand is not due to the importance of the metaphor in Smith's work but rather to the promotion of the metaphor by some later economists for their own ends.: Matches both key points — Smith barely used it, yet later economists promoted it for their own intellectual agenda. This is the passage's central claim. ✓
  • Choice B — Smith's metaphor of the invisible hand has been interpreted as a model of how individuals acting in their own interest produce aggregate benefits, but it was intended as a subtle critique of the economic theory of mercantilism.: Focuses on Smith's original intent versus modern interpretation, but ignores the passage's main point about how later economists manufactured the metaphor's fame. ✗
  • Choice C — Some twentieth-century economists gave Smith's metaphor of the invisible hand a significance it does not have in Smith's work, but it is nevertheless a useful model of how individuals produce aggregate benefits by pursuing their own economic interests.: Adds "nevertheless a useful model" — the passage makes no such concession. This contradicts the critical tone. ✗
  • Choice D — Although Smith is famed for his metaphor of the invisible hand, the metaphor was largely ignored until economists in the twentieth century came to realize that the metaphor was a robust model that anticipated their own views.: "Came to realize" misrepresents the passage — those economists were not discovering truth but were described as "eager to secure an intellectual pedigree for their views", implying self-interest, not genuine recognition. ✗
That matches Choice A — The reputation of Smith's metaphor of the invisible hand is not due to the importance of the metaphor in Smith's work but rather to the promotion of the metaphor by some later economists for their own ends..
